# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. The Ref is a hilarious and underrated Christmas movie

**Score:** 64
**Body:** Not sure how I missed this one but it's great. Phenomenal cast, razor sharp dialogue, laugh out loud funny. If you're looking for a dark comedy Christmas movie that isn't Bad Santa, this one won't disappoint. 

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I saw it a long time ago and I remember liking it.  I'll have to check it out again!
  - It holds up sharp jokes great cast and still funny on a rewatch
  - Worth a rewatch it still hits hard and the jokes land just as sharp
  - It holds up the jokes land hard and it feels mean in a fun way not many christmas movies go that route and pull it off like that
2. love this movie, Jingle All the Way is also criminally underrated!
  - Put that cookie down... NOW!!
  - My kid's first Arnold movie. He was so surprised and excited at the climax. It was incredibly cute.
    - this and Kindergarten Cop are really my favorite Arnold movies
3. Christine Beiranski is so hilarious!
4. One of the best Christmas movies that only about 7 people have seen. Welcome to the club!

## 2. Any suspense movie?

**Score:** 11
**Body:** 
I'm looking for thrillers that aren't too much focus on being spooky and jump scares. I rather have a good story or mystery that keeps you on your toes. I can't find much new movies myself, maybe I've seen most of the good ones already. 

Some movies that I really liked are:
Don't breathe,
Speak no evil,
Eden lake,
The invisible Man,
Taken (guess it's more action than thriller),
Heretic,
What keeps you alive,
The watcher

## 3. Need movie recommendations for thriller/crime

**Score:** 10
**Body:** I recently watched Now You See Me and the Oceans movies. I must say, this might be my new favorite movie genre. I need more movies like these!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Watch the original The Sting.  Thank me later.
  - 🔜✔WATCH HERE Movies r/demonslayerInfinityca/wiki/index/movie/
2. Inside Man (2006)  
  
The Italian Job (2003)  
  
Logan Lucky (2017)
3. The Sting

French Connection

Ronin   

To Live and Die in LA

Chaos

Basic

Heist

Chinatown

5. Three Days Of The Condor
6. Heat. De Niro vs Pachino.
7. Going in Style (2018 & og), The Thomas Crown Affair (99), Kiss Kiss Bang Bang, Bluestreak,  Entrapment, Point Break, The Asphalt Jungle (50), The Killing, The Taking of Pellham 123, 
Bad Grandmas,
Bad Girls (94),

9. Just so you know those movies are called heist movies.

The essential heist movies that all the other ones are influenced by are Rififi, Topkapi, and The Killing.

The first two were directed by Jules Dassin  and the last one was directed by Kubrick.

Another really good one that hasn't been mentioned yet is Michael Mann's Thief.

## 4. Best movies to watch in the last week of December?

**Score:** 10
**Body:** I’m planning to watch one movie every day during the last week of December.
Any must-watch recommendations?
Any genre is fine — just something worth ending the year with.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Metropolitan (1990)
2. Wings of Desire (1987)- sweeping epic love letter to the city of Berlin & a grand gorgeous romance 

Past Lives (2023) - love regret nostalgia and living your life 

Only Lovers Left Alive (2013) - super gorgeous and amazing soundtrack unlikely vampire story
3. The Green Mile
As Good as it Gets
No Country for Old Men 
Fargo 
Silence of the Lambs
Magnolia 
Thelma and Louise 
Duel
4. Lawrence of Arabia and What Dreams May Come.
5. Triple 9 (2016)

Reindeer Games (2000)
  - Can u take a moment an tell mw why these 2?  

Merry christmas
    - I like Gary Sinise a lot. Reindeer Games is a fun Christmas action/thriller. It's one of my favorites for Christmas time

I always recommend Triple 9. It was an under the radar gem(imo) cops/bank robbery action movie. And funny enough, both of these movies have an Affleck brother in them.
      - I will be checkin out i appreciate it
6. When Harry met Sally …classic new years movie
7. 2012 - send off a shitty year by watching the world end lol

**Score:** 8

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Macaulay Culkin Reveals Kevin’s Dad’s Job In ‘Home Alone’ After Decades Of Wild Theories Online

**Score:** 294

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. More like it's his own theory, and there's still no definitive answer. 

TLDR: he thinks he was a corporate lawyer.
  - For the mob.
    - They find out youre a lawyer and they immediately assume youre mobbed up.  Its a stereotype and its offensive.
2. The mom must have been in fashion due to number of mannequins they kept in their basement
  - It's their kink, don't be judgy.
  - I think this is the answer. There’s also a sewing machine and mannequins with dresses on in her bedroom.
3. “Corporate Lawyer”
  
Saved you the click.
4. In the novelization of the movie, which was released in 1991, it is said that Kevin's dad worked in Finance while his mother was a designer.
5. Always guessed he was a dentist because Kevin knew about the ADA.
6. Corporate Lawyer (so you don’t have to click and skim thru all the ads)

## 2. Your pick for a Jeffrey Epstein biopic?

**Score:** 217

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. If ever a film didn’t need to be made, it’s that one.
  - So Mark Wahlberg should do it?


"if I was on that island, it never would have gone down like that"
    - Nah, In 2025 to make a buck and push the change we all want to see you need diversity for these big hitting Hollywood productions.   

Cast Zendaya as Epstein.
  - I’m so tapped out on biopics.   Musician biopics are only slightly more bearable than criminal biopics.
  - Even Dick Cheney got a biopic, it’s only a matter of time
2. I think the only actor that can portray him with all the grace and dignity he deserves is Kathy Griffin.
3. Bob Odenkirk.
4. Titus Welliver (spelling?) may look the part. But not needed.
5. Saul goodman
6. Ron Perleman

## 3. Favorite box-office failure that later received the acclaim it truly deserved? I'll start

**Score:** 144
**Body:** The Shawshank Redemption flopped initially in 1994, but gained massive acclaim and cult status starting in 1995 as the top-rented video, fueled by its seven Oscar nominations.

2. Gattaca.
3. The Big Lebowski

5. Idocracy
6. Iron Giant

8. Office Space

10. Dredd

## 4. What are you gonna watch (rewatch) this Christmas season?

**Score:** 106
**Body:** James Stewart is one of my most favorite actors of all time and this movie is my comfort watch every Christmas (along with Home Alone and Tokyo Godfathers)

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Trading Places
  - Looking good Billy Ray!
2. Already watched Home Alone and A Christmas Story.
3. Die Hard, Bad Santa, Muppets, LOTR.
4. A Christmas Story
5. Big Fish
6. Die Hard, Coming to America, The Shining, the latest Fallout episode
7. The Family Man
8. The usuals. Elf, Die Hard, Bad Santa, Home Alone 1 & 2, The Grinch (both versions), Frozen, Christmas Chronicles, Muppets Christmas Carol, Christmas Vacation, Arthur's Perfect Christmas, Rudolph, Frosty, Little Drummer Boy, it's a Wonderful Life, The Santa Clause with Tim Allen, Jingle All The Way, Iron Man 3, Gremlins, Batman Returns......We Wish You a Turtle Christmas.
9. Watched it again this year… every year … the movie makes me sadder and sadder for George Bailey.

## 5. My all time favourite Christmas film, Santa Claus: The Movie

**Score:** 47
**Body:** Santa Claus: The Movie is my all-time favourite Christmas film and one of my all-time favourite films. If I get stranded on a desert island for Christmas and can only have one Christmas film, it's a no-brainer that I'm picking as to me. This is the perfect Christmas film. Seeing negative reviews of this film always breaks my heart as I watch it every Christmas. David Huddleston plays Santa with such heartfelt sincerity, to me, he is the best Santa Claus in film. Dudley Moore as Patch and John Lithgow as B.Z. are also huge highlights for this film. When you watch this film you can feel the sincerity and warmth on the screen. I can’t find anything to be critical of about this film as I think it’s a masterpiece of Christmas cinema and I will forever love it. If you're looking for the perfect Christmas movie, look no further. I highly recommend it.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. nothing ever looked as delicious as the food and the coke that Cornelia gave "The Boy".

One of my Allll Tiiime favorites!

I remember being annoyed when "the Santa Claus" (with tim allen) Came out and became the premier "santa" movie played on TV every year😞
2. John Lithgow in space freaked me out as a kid.
3. Mine is "The Night the Reindeer Died" starring Lee Majors.
4. The Super Duper Looper!!!
5. We’ll call it Christmas TWO!!!
6. #Puce
7. I loved this as a kid, but "Klaus" became my favorite Christmas movie after the first watch.
8. "Damn it, Towzer, get on with your story, stop giving me all these short sentences and making me go, uh-huh, uh-huh, uh-huh, like some kind of a damn moron."
9. Love this movie
10. Never seen this movie or even heard of it’s existence. Weird. I will suggest to watch it today for Christmas Day! Merry Christmas everyone!

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. The “trapped” sequence in Das Boot (1981) is the best depiction of a spiritual hell that I’ve ever seen

**Score:** 74
**Body:** SPOILERS. I ended up watching the 3.5 hour cut of a masterpiece of a submarine film called Das Boot from 1981. 

The sequence: there is an extended part of the film where they end up underwater and they’re trapped over 200 m underwater with nearly unbearable pressure. The entire sequence is absolutely stunning and shows a type of despair that eats 

away at any adult’s psyche. They’re supposed to be in that unbearable hopeless state for “6-8 hours”, but it stretches to 15 hours with little light. 

The moment where the sub finally breaches completely caught me off guard in that I didn’t expect to be moved so much. I’ve seen other excellent depictions of a “spiritual hell” (Shawshank Redemption, Dark Knight Rises, Excalibur, etc), but I think this one really takes the cake. I actually felt like a cathartic, almost religious level of relief as the the characters reacted to getting air for the first time. 

An unbelievable movie for those who have never seen it.

Scene: https://youtu.be/4ANbZsnjx9Q?si=gOGgOEWU\_pLcDA0i

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I was also incredibly moved by that sequence because I do sense a spiritual cadence during it. What makes Das Boot such an effective war film is how the sense of fear and dread permeates through the psyche of the characters as the line between life and death increasingly becomes blurred.

The submarine a vessel designed to keep them alive in an inhospitable environment gradually becomes their own coffin and all of this culminates to the "trapped" sequence. The worst has seemingly happened, the men are as good as dead in what appears to be a state of purgatory.

The Captain sits sullenly as he receives the news of one failure after another, he appears unbothered as he's accepted his fate and so have the rest of the crew. Yet they keep working despite completely aware that the next minute could very well be their last. And by some miracle they persevere against all odds, they don't react with jubilant cheers their first reaction once they surface is to take a gasp of fresh air, because they're still alive after all. It's what made the ending so painful in this film.
  - If you read the accounts, filming was a very unpleasant experience although their U boat stage was on dry land. It was a full replica set so as the camera moved through the submarine the cast often found that they couldn't get out. They were stuck. It was hot from the lights and the ventilation was poor.

## 2. Revolutionary Theory vs. Praxis. Sensei is the real reveloutionary in One Battle After Another

**Score:** 44
**Body:** The French 75 treat revolution as a performance. Their operations are dangerous, but they’re structured around buzzwords, procedures, secret questions, and an almost bureaucratic obsession with proving allegiance. The emphasis is less on what the revolution materially does and more on whether it is being performed correctly.

Perfida is central to this idea. She believes in the cause, but she refuses to discipline her desires around it. Her insistence on having sex during missions undercuts the seriousness of the revolutionary performance, not by rejecting it, but by exposing how fragile it is. She’s willing to disrupt timing and cohesion for personal gratification, revealing how quickly her ideology gives way to impulse. That same logic carries through her sabotaging missions for her own ends and eventually becoming an informant. When pressure hits, the revolutionary language collapses and self-preservation takes over. The same thing happens with the other captured members, who drop the rhetoric once it becomes materially inconvenient.

Sensei exists as a direct contrast. He doesn’t speak in slogans or perform revolutionary identity. As a Latino, resistance isn’t a role he steps into, it’s part of everyday life. His network is complex and disciplined, but it’s built on trust, familiarity, and necessity rather than passwords or ideological tests.

The movies shows that as the years have went by, he revolution has thinned into even more pure process, embodied by Comrade Josh obsessively maintaining the secret question while refusing to help Bob. In the same moment, Sensei helps without hesitation or questioning.

The film isn’t saying one group is brave and the other isn’t. It’s showing how revolutionary theory hardens into cliché when it becomes about form instead of function. The French 75 practice revolution as an identity, complete with rules, rituals, and contradictions. Sensei practices it as necessity. One is constantly tested and performed and the other just operates. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. 100% - there's also a direct comparison drawn between the French 75 raiding the detention center (not enough room for everyone on the truck) vs. Sensei's clear intentions of \*everyone\* getting out safely and operating as a community.
2. I think you totally missed that Sommerville is a part of the same border operation as Sensei. right before he's captured, we see him working the Mexico side of it, and the interrogator makes the connection when he briefs Lockjaw.

given that he's the one who sent Bob & Willa to Batkan Cross and that his radio station is part of the French 75 network, we can assume that the F75 is not just aware of but actively allied with Sensei, with at least some degree of co-ordination.

you're right about the contrast drawn between Sensei vs Perfidia & Comrade Josh, but they don't represent the entire French 75 – Josh even gets reprimanded by his supervisor for being a nitpicker! we don't see exactly what the modern-day F75 are up to now, but all of the surviving OG members are still involved and presumably doing actual important work
3. I do find these type of takes quite a bit reductive because it really isnt as simple as that and part of the value in this work is both the contradictions of PTA politics and what is told and show across the movie.

But i dont think the French 75 are that much of a joke, or that they didnt matter at all and they were only by performance.  Because if that was the take from PTA then it doesnt make sense that Sensei to even had an admiration from them if that was the case or that Willa ends up using their radio in order to assist at a rally or that Deandra, Howard, the nuns or Bob old pal still are active agents in the fight.

But even more, the plan of the ex french 75 to save Willa does help and was working if it wasnt for Willa own naivity that ends up giving them clues to the army.

You are right that they do end up becoming swapped away in the performance of Perfidia lust (and that in itself is quite a choice from PTA, who seem uncapable of let sexual drive take a seatback across his world) but i think the categorization of them as just a performance group is a simplification of what happened.
4. Yes, Sensei does the more quotidian but more effective revolutionary work. To PTA’s credit, he’s the one major character that is mostly his own creation, rather than Pynchon’s (although he shares some traits with a couple of characters on the more surreal side of Vineland that PTA does not otherwise address in OBAA).
  - i mean, according to PTA himself it was a collaboration with Benicio so both should get a credit for that character.
5. I don't necessarily disagree with your conclusions, but I think the fact that this was your takeaway from the movie shows why many black leftists found it extremely offensive. Like this:

>Sensei exists as a direct contrast. He doesn’t speak in slogans or perform revolutionary identity. As a Latino, resistance isn’t a role he steps into, it’s part of everyday life.

is just ridiculous. You don't think resistance is also internal to black people? The entirety of US society is built upon black people being at the very bottom. The one group of people who can't gain entry to white identity is black people. Yet this movie frames black resistance as entirely unserious and performative, and leaves the audience to think it's others who are actually oppressed and doing resistance right. Which is extremely ironic given that arguably the most famous US revolutionary group, the Black Panthers, explicitly condemned actions similar to what the French 75 do in the movie (the Weathermen were a group around the same time committing similar acts which iirc it was Fred Hampton who said what they were doing was reckless and didn't serve to actually forward revolution). It's even more telling because in the book the movie is loosely based on, the Perfida character is a white woman. PTA basically created a mockery of black revolutionary activity for...what? To work out his issues on screen with his daughter? 

Basically, the racial politics of this movie are really messed up.
  - I don’t think he really makes any commentary on black revolutionists. The main driver behind the revolution is the treatment and detention of immigrants. Just because some members of French 75 were black doesn’t mean they’re a representation of black resistance.
    - Eh, I do see the poster point tho. It did kind of show it as sort of a black led group, which was half BPP and half Weatherman. Which shows how much PTA just wanted this as backdrop and not anything too serious because those two groups had radically different tactics and the BPP thought the latter were just wacky.
  - this was my main issue with the film tbh. the racial politics are lame, and we’re asked to ignore them because “it’s not about that”. but sensei is positioned to be espoused, and a demonstration of what activism should really look like. meanwhile black folks are the performative foil being radical but ineffective. but the projects of our people are also drastically different, not to mention all the various other issues here. 
6. A lot of it is symbolism for how different groups were approaching revolution at different times.

The SDS was closely allied with the yippies. Sex drugs and rock and roll wasn't a distraction; it was a component of toppling the corporate war machine. If people find a better way than conspicuous consumption, a better values system, the revolution will succeed. They thought. See *Children of Men* for how the sexually charged revolution hippy is impotent.

Mexico has a complex history of revolution. Its older and comes from many angles. It runs deeper and is based on more community trust built over generations than passwords. And it's not an exciting sexy thing; its older than everyone's grandpa.

The film requires a kind of alt history for our current time because mass surveillance will end any of the styles of revolution lovingly shown. Passwords and sleeper cells will not work. So it kind of mocks them.

## 3. The Night of the Generals (1967) Authority, Ambiguity, and Moral Reckoning

**Score:** 10
**Body:** Re‑watching The Night of the Generals has left me pondering how the film blends a whodunit with a critique of military obedience. 

Anderson’s “dual‑track” narrative, alternating the police investigation with flashbacks of each general’s wartime actions, forces us to question not only who committed the murders but why the chain of command can shield such crimes.   
  
The recurring visual of shadows behind pristine uniforms reinforces the tension between order and moral decay.    


Do you think the ending offers a true resolution, or does it bow to the constraints of 1960s Hollywood?   
How does the film’s structure affect our empathy for the suspects, and can we draw parallels to modern debates on command responsibility?  

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 4. Looking for insightful book/s on cinema

**Score:** 4
**Body:** Hello everyone,

I have been browsing through several best-of lists of books on and about cinema and I would like to have your opinion on the subject. To wit: I am not looking for a very serious, in-depth history of world cinema. But, as much as I enjoyed reading "Hollywood Babylon" as a teenager, I do not want a full-on, no-holds-barred exposé either.

Is there a book about classic Hollywood cinema that manages to cover both serious analysis and insightful examples? For instance, I read an article last week about how John Wayne started to use toupees when he turned a certain age. I do not consider that remark as an example of mean, idle gossip but a serious remark that tells about Hollywood's strict regimentation on the representation of human beauty.

Could anyone please help me with my query?

Thanks in advance for your help and attention.



### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Peter Bogdanovich books are serious without being academic:  *Who the Hell Made it?* (and there are others). David Thomson also good. Richard Jewell. Survey the British Film Institute site.
2. I put together a list of recommended book here:

[http://www.repfilms.com/film\_books/](http://www.repfilms.com/film_books/)

I need to add a bunch more, especially this one

[https://www.upress.umn.edu/9780816670109/the-genius-of-the-system/](https://www.upress.umn.edu/9780816670109/the-genius-of-the-system/)
3. *The Devil’s Candy*, *Final Cut* and *Picture* (by Lillian Ross) are all great behind-the-scenes books. *The Kid Stays in the Picture* has something of the Hollywood Babylon spirit, in the same sense that a Kentucky bourbon has something of the ethanol spirit. David Thomson’s *Biographical Dictionary of Film* is a must-have, and his *Have You Seen?* is a great go-to after watching a movie. You might also enjoy the *Projections* series from Faber which are all edited and written by industry figures. All these can be found super cheap - building a good library of cinema books is a very affordable goal.

## 5. Am I the only one who felt sympathy for Mr. Potter's wheelchair pusher on "It's A Wonderful Life"?

**Score:** 4
**Body:** I personally felt Mr. Potter's wheelchair pusher was a very underappreciated, undervalued and a person who doesn't get much love in life and deserved a lot more than what he's getting, I wish the wheelchair pusher would've snapped over Mr. Potter stealing the $8,000 he took from Uncle Billy and give back the money along with apologizing to them for the distress they went through.

I wish the wheelchair pusher had a happy ending as he definitely deserved to have one.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Sounds like we're finally getting the fanfic no one's been waiting for!

(I jest. I love that movie. The other day I was thinking, wouldn't it have been something if when George looks for Mary after he's "made his wish" she and Violet had gotten together...)
  - I also love the movie but would've liked to see a happy ending for Potter's lackey, I feel he deserved to have one.
    - > would've liked to see a happy ending for Potter's lackey, I feel he deserved to have one.

There is an interesting parallel scene in 2001's Hannibal.   I think it's my favorite "lackey getting an opportunity to get back at his tormentor" scene, though I guess it's not a huge genre to choose from.   I wonder how one would feel on down the years, having made that snap decision.   The question shifts from whether the tormentor "deserved" it and more to what the act of retribution does to *us*.
2. I just saw it for the first time this year and I was anticipating he would do exactly what you wrote. The actual ending works better though because it shows all the good George did in his life and how that sort of friendship is the one thing Potter can't buy.

---
